name: Group Personality Compatibility for Joint Choice
core_claim: This is not a single personality instrument but a design pattern: a method for combining TWO individual personality (or personality-derived preference) profiles into ONE joint recommendation target so a couple/dyad can find a show both want to watch. The claim is that the way you combine two people's signals materially changes joint satisfaction, and that the right combination function is not obvious. Three families exist. (1) AGGREGATE-PREFERENCE models: convert each person's personality to a content-preference prior, then merge the two priors with a social-choice aggregation function (Average, Least-Misery, Most-Pleasure, Average-Without-Misery, Fairness/Multiplicative). (2) DIRECT-PROFILE-COMBINATION models: compute a feature of the personality PAIR itself (trait similarity/distance, complementarity, dominance/assertiveness asymmetry) and use it to modulate aggregation or predict dyad satisfaction directly. (3) SOCIAL-DYNAMICS models: use personality to simulate how the two people would actually negotiate the choice in the room (conflict-resolution style, who concedes, tie strength), then predict the choice that real-world negotiation would land on. The theoretical link to viewing enjoyment is twofold: media-preference theory (traits shape what gratifications each person seeks) plus group-decision theory (the joint outcome depends on the aggregation/negotiation function, not just the two underlying tastes). For HelpME2C this construct IS the differentiator — the North Star is two people discovering a show they BOTH want, so the combination function is the load-bearing part of the engine, not an afterthought.
predictive_validity_for_media: Evidence is strongest for the aggregation-strategy choice and weaker for direct personality-pair compatibility predicting JOINT media enjoyment specifically. Aggregation strategies: the group-recommender literature consistently finds Average (AVG) and Least-Misery (LM) are the two dominant score-aggregation strategies, with 'no universally winning strategy' (Masthoff) — LM tends to do better at avoiding a strongly dissatisfied member and is favoured for small groups where one unhappy member dooms the joint watch, while AVG maximises mean satisfaction; for a two-person joint-watch goal LM-style logic ('something neither hates') is the better theoretical fit but this is a design argument, not a large quantitative win. Personality-guided aggregation beats naive strategies: PEGA (arXiv:2304.08851) reports 77.6%-169% Recall@20 improvement over predefined AVG/LM and 54%+ NDCG@20 over rule-based personality methods — but these are offline benchmarks on review datasets, not couple joint-watch satisfaction, so external validity to HelpME2C is uncertain. [uncertain] Direct personality-pair -> satisfaction: the strongest empirical signal comes from DYAD RELATIONSHIP satisfaction (Frontiers, fpsyg.2018.01009, five studies): profile DISTANCE (dissimilarity) predicts lower satisfaction with substantial betas (-0.29 to -0.77) once POSITIVITY (frequency of extreme-positive self-ratings) is controlled, and distance+positivity act as reciprocal suppressors. This is medium-to-large effect but for RELATIONSHIP satisfaction, not joint media enjoyment — the transfer to 'two people enjoy the same show' is inferential. [uncertain] Conflict-resolution-style and trust-weighted models (Quijano-Sanchez HappyMovie/ARISE) show personality+trust+memory improves group movie-rec satisfaction over plain aggregation in user studies, but small samples. Net: the AGGREGATION-FUNCTION choice has solid (if domain-transferred) support; personality COMPATIBILITY as a joint-enjoyment predictor is plausible but thinly evidenced for media specifically.
instrument_design: There is no separate questionnaire to administer for this construct — it consumes the per-person personality profile produced by whatever onboarding instrument is chosen (e.g. the 6-item Openness/Extraversion/Neuroticism BFI-10/TIPI subset recommended in the big-five-neo-pi item) and adds a combination LAYER. The 'instrument' here is the aggregation/compatibility function plus, optionally, a tiny amount of extra elicitation. Minimum viable additions for a two-person flow: (a) ZERO extra items if using pure preference-prior aggregation (AVG / LM / AVG-without-misery over the two derived priors) — this is the lowest-friction path; (b) optionally 1-2 items per person to capture conflict/decision style ('When you and your partner can't agree on what to watch, do you usually give in, push for your pick, or look for a third option?') if implementing a social-dynamics weighting; (c) optionally a single relationship/tie-strength item ('How often do you watch together?') to gate how aggressively to enforce least-misery. The combination math itself is cheap: distance = mean absolute trait difference; positivity = derivable from extreme responses; assertiveness asymmetry ~ Extraversion gap; cooperativeness ~ Agreeableness level (Thomas-Kilmann mapping: Agreeableness->cooperativeness, Extraversion->assertiveness). For under-2-minute onboarding, the right answer is pure preference-prior aggregation with no extra items, reserving 1 optional decision-style item for a v2.
cold_start_applicability: Highly applicable and arguably MORE valuable at cold start than at warm state, because this is exactly the moment where the system has two people, zero shared viewing history, and no co-watch interaction log to learn the joint function empirically. Personality-derived priors for each person can be combined at t=0 to produce a joint prior immediately — the only cold-start mechanism that works for a brand-new DYAD (collaborative-filtering group methods need each member's history; personality does not). Signal quality inherits the weakness of the underlying personality->preference link (small trait-genre correlations, |r| mostly <0.30), so the JOINT prior is also a weak, broad-strokes tilt rather than a confident joint ranker — but it is strictly better than recommending the popularity baseline to a couple, because it can at least exclude content one member's prior strongly rejects (least-misery flooring). Cross-medium: works in principle, since the combination operates on medium-agnostic preference vectors; a Korean-drama-leaning person and an anime-leaning person can be merged on shared theme dimensions. The honest framing: personality gives a usable DAY-ONE joint prior that the first few co-watch signals should rapidly overwrite; its enduring value is (1) the cold-start dyad seed and (2) a tie-breaker/diversity-and-fairness shaper once warm. [uncertain]
group_compatibility_use: THIS IS THE WHOLE ITEM, so the recommendation is concrete. Default architecture for HelpME2C: aggregate at the PREFERENCE-PRIOR level (convert each person's traits to a theme/genre prior first, THEN combine) rather than averaging raw trait scores — averaging trait scores is psychologically meaningless (the mean of two people's Openness is not a person), whereas averaging two preference vectors is well-defined. Default aggregation function: AVERAGE-WITHOUT-MISERY (AVG ranking with a veto floor that removes any title strongly disliked by either member), which operationalises 'a show we BOTH want' better than pure AVG (can recommend a polarising title) or pure LM (can recommend bland mutually-tolerated mediocrity). LM-with-floor is the close runner-up and the safer choice when the two profiles are far apart. Personality-pair signals to layer on top, in priority order: (1) PROFILE DISTANCE as a confidence/strategy switch — when the two personality profiles are similar, AVG is safe and the joint prior is sharp; when they are far apart, lean harder on the misery floor and widen diversity, and lower confidence in the joint prior (Frontiers dyad evidence: distance lowers joint satisfaction). (2) ASSERTIVENESS/DOMINANCE asymmetry (Extraversion gap) to optionally up-weight the less-flexible member's vetoes so the dominant personality doesn't steamroll the choice — this is the 'alliances' / conflict-resolution-style insight from Quijano-Sanchez and the PEGA 'stubborn vs easy-going' weighting, which beat naive aggregation in benchmarks. (3) COOPERATIVENESS (Agreeableness) to predict who will accommodate. Evidence ranking for WHICH combination predicts joint satisfaction: aggregation-strategy choice is well-studied (AVG/LM dominant, no universal winner, LM/AVG-without-misery favoured for small groups and misery-avoidance); personality-WEIGHTED aggregation outperforms naive aggregation in offline benchmarks (PEGA, NCF-Big5) and small user studies (HappyMovie); profile SIMILARITY (low distance) predicting higher joint satisfaction is well-evidenced for relationships but only inferentially for media. AVOID: (a) trait-score averaging, (b) trusting a complementarity ('opposites attract') model — the dyad literature finds far more evidence for similarity-helps than complementarity-helps, except possibly on a dominance axis. Recommended HelpME2C combination: per-person prior -> AVG-without-misery aggregation -> modulated by profile-distance (strategy switch) and assertiveness-asymmetry (veto weighting), with the joint prior treated as a weak day-one seed.
quiz_ux_implications: The combination layer adds little direct quiz surface (it consumes the existing personality items), so the UX implications are mostly about FRAMING and about the optional decision-style item. Framing: present the two-person step as 'getting your shared picks right' rather than 'measuring your compatibility' — a compatibility score shown to a couple is socially loaded (people read it as a relationship verdict) and risks both social-desirability distortion and genuine relational harm, so DO NOT surface a numeric couple-compatibility score; use the distance signal silently to shape recommendations. If adding the optional conflict/decision-style item, forced-choice scenario framing ('When you can't agree, you usually: give in / hold out / find a third thing') is far better than Likert agreement — it is naturally gamified, low-friction, and ipsative formats reduce acquiescence. Faking risk is low (no incentive to fake a recommendation quiz) but PERFORMATIVE answering in front of a partner is a real risk if both people answer on the same device or can see each other's answers — administer personality items PRIVATELY per person (separate sessions/links), never as a shared visible quiz, both for honesty and to avoid relational friction. Acquiescence and extreme-response styles still bias the underlying trait scores and therefore the distance computation; reverse-keyed items in the base instrument mitigate this. Keep the two-person addition to 0-2 items.
cross_cultural_validity: Two layers of validity concern. First, the combination layer inherits the cross-cultural fragility of the underlying personality instrument — if Openness is the most translation-fragile Big-Five factor and HelpME2C's Hindi/Arabic-content audiences fall in lower trait-congruence zones, then profile-DISTANCE between two people is computed on noisier per-person estimates and the distance signal degrades accordingly; a cross-cultural DYAD (two people from different cultural/response-style backgrounds) is especially problematic because differential extreme-vs-midpoint responding inflates apparent personality distance that is really a response-style artefact, not true dissimilarity. Second, the social-dynamics assumptions are themselves culturally loaded: conflict-resolution norms, who is expected to accommodate, and the acceptability of one partner vetoing differ markedly across cultures (collectivist vs individualist decision norms, gendered deference expectations), so a hard-coded assertiveness-weighting or 'dominant member' model risks encoding a Western dyadic-negotiation assumption that misfires for some user pairs. [uncertain] Mitigations: use professionally localised personality items (not machine translation); normalise/centre responses per respondent to dampen response-style-driven false distance; keep the social-dynamics weighting conservative and optional rather than a hard rule; and lower confidence in the joint personality prior for cross-cultural pairs and lower-congruence locales, leaning more on explicit shared seed picks instead.
privacy_consent_implications: Higher sensitivity than single-user personality collection because the combination step processes TWO people's profiles together and can generate a relational inference (a couple-compatibility/distance signal), which is both special-category-adjacent (Neuroticism->mental-health, plus a relationship-quality inference) and a SECOND-PARTY data concern: person A should not be shown person B's raw personality/Neuroticism scores, and person B must independently consent to their data being used to shape A's (and the joint) recommendations. GDPR posture: explicit opt-in consent from EACH member for personality processing and for joint use; purpose-limited; profiling under Article 22-adjacent scrutiny since it shapes what each is shown. Article 9 risk arises if Neuroticism or a relational-distress inference is stored — prefer to compute the joint prior and the distance modulation transiently and store only the derived joint content-prior vector, not raw trait scores and NOT a persisted compatibility score (a stored 'relationship compatibility: low' record is a sensitive, re-identifying, potentially harmful inference). Per-person hard-deletability on account deletion, and a member must be able to revoke participation in the shared profile without deleting the partner's account. This is squarely an ADR-0012 / Stop-and-Ask zone for HelpME2C: two-party consent, no surfaced compatibility score, transient/minimal storage.
relationship_to_theme_vocabulary: The combination layer does not map to new theme slugs directly; it OPERATES ON the per-person theme priors (which the underlying personality construct maps to) and produces a joint theme prior. The relevant patterns: (1) INTERSECTION/overlap of the two members' high-affinity themes is the safe joint core — e.g. if both index toward found-family, mystery-investigation, slow-burn, that overlap is the strongest joint signal. (2) The MISERY-FLOOR removes themes one member strongly rejects even if the other loves them — e.g. one partner's existential-horror / cosmic-horror / toxic-relationship / war-and-its-toll affinity is suppressed if the other's profile (e.g. high Neuroticism + low sensation-seeking) strongly avoids distressing content; this asymmetric-suppression is where the two-person model adds the most value over single-user recs. (3) BRIDGE themes for distant pairs: when profile distance is high (e.g. high-Openness slow-burn/nonlinear-storytelling/earned-ambiguity person paired with low-Openness whodunit/heist-and-caper/underdog-rise person), the joint prior should up-weight cross-appeal middle-ground themes — character-study, redemption-arc, ensemble-mosaic, dark-comedy, coming-of-age tend to satisfy across the Openness gap better than either extreme. (4) Assertiveness-weighting (Extraversion gap) shifts the joint prior toward the more-dominant member's themes unless veto-protected. These are directional, small-effect priors used for shaping the joint shortlist and applying the misery floor, not confident per-title joint ranking. [uncertain]
implementation_recommendation: INCLUDE — this combination layer is effectively mandatory for HelpME2C because the two-person joint recommendation IS the product. Recommendation: implement at the PREFERENCE-PRIOR level (never average raw trait scores). Pipeline: (1) per person, derive a theme/genre prior from the onboarding personality subset (Openness/Extraversion[/Neuroticism]); (2) combine the two priors with AVERAGE-WITHOUT-MISERY (AVG ranking + veto floor removing titles either prior strongly rejects) as the default joint strategy, with LM-with-floor as the fallback when profile distance is high; (3) modulate with two personality-pair signals — profile DISTANCE (mean absolute trait difference) as a confidence/strategy switch and to widen diversity for distant pairs, and ASSERTIVENESS asymmetry (Extraversion gap, optionally a 1-item decision-style elicitation) as an optional veto-weighting so the dominant personality doesn't steamroll; (4) treat the whole joint prior as a WEAK day-one seed that co-watch interactions overwrite. Phase advice: ship v1 as pure AVG-without-misery preference-prior aggregation with NO extra quiz items and NO surfaced compatibility score (lowest friction, lowest privacy risk, biggest share of the available signal). Defer personality-WEIGHTED aggregation (PEGA/Quijano-Sanchez style) and the optional decision-style item to v2, gated on real co-watch satisfaction data to validate that weighting actually helps for couples (offline-benchmark gains may not transfer). Hard constraints: administer personality items PRIVATELY per person; two-party explicit consent; store only the derived joint prior, never raw scores or a compatibility verdict; ADR-0012 review required. Dependencies: entirely dependent on a per-person personality (or other preference-prior) instrument existing — this item has no standalone value without one. North-Star trace: the combination function is the single most direct lever on 'a show two people both want to watch', so getting the aggregation right (misery-floor over naive averaging) is the highest-leverage piece of the cold-start engine. This packages the moat-relevant logic that lives in packages/ml — touching it is a Stop-and-Ask boundary.
key_sources: Masthoff, J. 'Group Recommender Systems: Aggregation, Satisfaction and Group Attributes', in Recommender Systems Handbook (Springer) https://link.springer.com/chapter/10.1007/978-1-4899-7637-6_22 (PAYWALLED; earlier 'Group Recommender Systems: Combining Individual Models' freely available via Semantic Scholar https://www.semanticscholar.org/paper/72098ce47046693158671ed605a884550b3e4f37 and lecture slides https://pro.unibz.it/projects/schoolrecsys17/JudithMasthoff.pdf FREE). Wang et al., 'A Personality-Guided Preference Aggregator for Ephemeral Group Recommendation' (PEGA), arXiv:2304.08851 https://arxiv.org/html/2304.08851v2 (FREE; published Applied Soft Computing https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S1568494624010482 PAYWALLED). Quijano-Sanchez, L. et al., 'Social Factors in Group Recommender Systems' / 'Using Personality to Create Alliances in Group Recommender Systems' / HappyMovie & ARISE — Social Factors PDF FREE https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Lara-Quijano-Sanchez/publication/262253429_Social_Factors_in_Group_Recommender_Systems and architecture paper https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s10489-013-0504-y (PAYWALLED). Weidmann, Schoenbrodt et al., 'Similarity and Positivity of Personality Profiles Consistently Predict Relationship Satisfaction in Dyads', Frontiers in Psychology 2018, 9:1009 https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/psychology/articles/10.3389/fpsyg.2018.01009/full (FREE, open access). Delic et al., 'Group recommender systems for tourism: how does personality predict preferences...', User Modeling and User-Adapted Interaction 2023 https://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11257-023-09361-2 (PAYWALLED). 'A Preliminary Study of the Impact of Personality on Satisfaction in Group Contexts' (groups of size two, tie strength) ACM https://dl.acm.org/doi/fullHtml/10.1145/3631700.3664893 (FREE HTML). Wood & Bell, 'Predicting interpersonal conflict resolution styles from personality characteristics' (Big-Five -> Thomas-Kilmann), Personality and Individual Differences 2008 https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/abs/pii/S0191886908000998 (PAYWALLED). 'Predicting Group Choices from Group Profiles', arXiv:2308.03083 https://arxiv.org/pdf/2308.03083 (FREE).
